steps.instruction: Reading a line: y = mx + b

slides.title: Every line is y = mx + b
slides.body: A straight line can be written as y = mx + b. The two letters m and b tell you everything you need to draw it.

slides.title: b is the y-intercept
slides.body: b is where the line crosses the y-axis (when x = 0). In y = 2x + 1, b = 1, so the line passes through the point (0, 1).

slides.title: m is the slope
slides.body: m is the slope — how steep the line is. It means 'rise over run': go up m units for every 1 unit you move right. In y = 2x + 1, m = 2, so from (0, 1) you go up 2 and right 1 to reach (1, 3).

explanations.correct: Great — now let's read m and b off some graphs!

steps.instruction: The line shows y = 2x + 1. Type the y-intercept (where it crosses the y-axis).

explanations.correct: The y-intercept is 1 — that's where the line crosses the y-axis (x = 0).
explanations.wrong_intercept: Not quite. The y-intercept is the y-value where x = 0. Read it off the graph.
explanations.incomplete: Type a whole number for the y-intercept.
explanations.generic_wrong: In y = mx + b, the number b is the y-intercept.

steps.instruction: For y = 2x − 4, type the x-intercept (where the line crosses the x-axis).

explanations.wrong_intercept: Not quite. The x-intercept is the x-value where y = 0. Read it off the graph.
explanations.incomplete: Type a whole number for the x-intercept.
explanations.generic_wrong: Set y = 0 and solve: 0 = 2x − 4, so x = 2.

steps.instruction: Find intercepts using only the equation

slides.title: y-intercept: set x = 0
slides.body: The y-intercept is where the line touches the y-axis. That always happens when x = 0. In y = mx + b, plug in x = 0 and you get y = b. So the y-intercept is just b — the number with no x!

slides.title: x-intercept: set y = 0
slides.body: The x-intercept is where the line touches the x-axis, which is when y = 0. Set y = 0 and solve for x. Example: y = 2x − 6 → 0 = 2x − 6 → 2x = 6 → x = 3.

explanations.correct: Now try finding intercepts without a graph!

steps.instruction: No graph this time! For y = 3x + 5, find the y-intercept using the equation.

explanations.correct: Exactly! Set x = 0: y = 3(0) + 5 = 5. The y-intercept is b = 5.
explanations.wrong_intercept: Set x = 0. In y = 3x + 5 that leaves y = 5 — the number b.
explanations.incomplete: Type a whole number for the y-intercept.
explanations.generic_wrong: The y-intercept is the b in y = mx + b.

steps.instruction: No graph! For y = 2x − 6, find the x-intercept using the equation.

explanations.incomplete: Type a whole number for the x-intercept.
explanations.generic_wrong: To find the x-intercept, set y = 0 and solve for x.

steps.instruction: Plot the line y = x + 2. Place 2 dots, click one of them again to unplace it.

explanations.correct: Perfect! Both dots sit on y = x + 2 — for example (0, 2) and (1, 3).
explanations.incomplete: Place 2 dots on the line. Click a dot again to remove it.
explanations.generic_wrong: Each point must satisfy y = x + 2. Try (0, 2): y = 0 + 2 = 2.

steps.instruction: Plot the line y = 2x − 1. Place 2 dots, click one of them again to unplace it.

explanations.correct: Nice! Both dots are on y = 2x − 1 — for example (0, −1) and (1, 1).
explanations.incomplete: Place 2 dots on the line. Click a dot again to remove it.
explanations.generic_wrong: Each point must satisfy y = 2x − 1. Try (0, −1): y = 2(0) − 1 = −1.
